comment_text.sn16.13:1.4: Asked by Bhaddāli at MN 65:29.1. There, the Buddha said, as he did to Sāriputta at Bu Pj 1:3.4.6, that decline comes from defiling influences in the Saṅgha. See too the account of the monk Subhadda, whose relief at the Buddha’s passing prompted Mahākassapa to organize the First Council (DN 16:6.20.1).
comment_text.sn16.13:1.8: In an age of commercialized spirituality, counterfeit translations and fake Buddhas generated by AI, proliferation of sects and gurus, and collapse of moral authority of traditional Saṅgha under the weight of institutional corruption, this seems relevant.
comment_text.sn16.13:2.1: Jātarūpa is the natural, unworked gold, for which the technical term is “native gold” (see AN 3.70:38.4, AN 3.101:1.1). I believe jātarūpa stems from the common Vedic sense of rūpa as “form” or “manifestation” of the gods. Jāta is what is “born” or what is part of the “natural” order. Thus jātarūpa is the manifestation of the divine form (of the sun) in nature.
comment_text.sn16.13:2.2: Kauṭilya details the regulation of the various kinds of gold and its adulterants in Arthaśāstra 2.13 and 2.14.
comment_text.sn16.13:3.1: Disruptions in the elements create runaway climate change that makes life, and religious practice, impossible on earth (MN 28, AN 7.66).
comment_text.sn16.13:4.3: All four wings of the dispensation are essential. | Meditative immersion is already included in the threefold training of ethics, immersion, and wisdom, but is specially emphasized here. | Today, much of Buddhism is deficient in these factors. The order of nuns is rejected by some communities dominated by a male Saṅgha. The Buddha has become a supernatural icon to be worshiped, while study of his teaching in the Suttas is rarely pursued. Monastic discipline is honored more in the breach than the observance. The practice of meditation is popular in some circles, but much of it aims at therapeutic outcomes rather than deep immersion, while some schools reject immersion altogether.

root_text.sn16.13:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn16.13:1.3: Atha kho āyasmā mahākassapo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā mahākassapo bhagavantaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn16.13:1.4: “ko nu kho, bhante, hetu ko paccayo, yena pubbe appatarāni ceva sikkhāpadāni ahesuṁ bahutarā ca bhikkhū aññāya saṇṭhahiṁsu?
root_text.sn16.13:1.5: Ko pana, bhante, hetu ko paccayo, yenetarahi bahutarāni ceva sikkhāpadāni appatarā ca bhikkhū aññāya saṇṭhahantī”ti?
root_text.sn16.13:1.6: “Evañcetaṁ, kassapa, hoti sattesu hāyamānesu saddhamme antaradhāyamāne, bahutarāni ceva sikkhāpadāni honti appatarā ca bhikkhū aññāya saṇṭhahanti.
root_text.sn16.13:1.7: Na tāva, kassapa, saddhammassa antaradhānaṁ hoti yāva na saddhammappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati.
root_text.sn16.13:1.8: Yato ca kho, kassapa, saddhammappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati, atha saddhammassa antaradhānaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn16.13:2.1: Seyyathāpi, kassapa, na tāva jātarūpassa antaradhānaṁ hoti yāva na jātarūpappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati.
root_text.sn16.13:2.2: Yato ca kho, kassapa, jātarūpappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati, atha kho jātarūpassa antaradhānaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn16.13:2.3: Evameva kho, kassapa, na tāva saddhammassa antaradhānaṁ hoti yāva na saddhammappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati.
root_text.sn16.13:2.4: Yato ca kho, kassapa, saddhammappatirūpakaṁ loke uppajjati, atha saddhammassa antaradhānaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn16.13:3.1: Na kho, kassapa, pathavīdhātu saddhammaṁ antaradhāpeti, na āpodhātu saddhammaṁ antaradhāpeti, na tejodhātu saddhammaṁ antaradhāpeti, na vāyodhātu saddhammaṁ antaradhāpeti;
root_text.sn16.13:3.2: atha kho idheva te uppajjanti moghapurisā ye imaṁ saddhammaṁ antaradhāpenti.
root_text.sn16.13:3.4: na kho, kassapa, evaṁ saddhammassa antaradhānaṁ hoti.
root_text.sn16.13:4.1: Pañca khome, kassapa, okkamaniyā dhammā saddhammassa sammosāya antaradhānāya saṁvattanti.
root_text.sn16.13:4.3: Idha, kassapa, bhikkhū bhikkhuniyo upāsakā upāsikāyo satthari agāravā viharanti appatissā, dhamme agāravā viharanti appatissā, saṅghe agāravā viharanti appatissā, sikkhāya agāravā viharanti appatissā, samādhismiṁ agāravā viharanti appatissā—
root_text.sn16.13:4.4: ime kho, kassapa, pañca okkamaniyā dhammā saddhammassa sammosāya antaradhānāya saṁvattanti.
root_text.sn16.13:5.1: Pañca khome, kassapa, dhammā saddhammassa ṭhitiyā asammosāya anantaradhānāya saṁvattanti.
root_text.sn16.13:5.3: Idha, kassapa, bhikkhū bhikkhuniyo upāsakā upāsikāyo satthari sagāravā viharanti sappatissā, dhamme sagāravā viharanti sappatissā, saṅghe sagāravā viharanti sappatissā, sikkhāya sagāravā viharanti sappatissā, samādhismiṁ sagāravā viharanti sappatissā—
root_text.sn16.13:5.4: ime kho, kassapa, pañca dhammā saddhammassa ṭhitiyā asammosāya anantaradhānāya saṁvattantī”ti.

translation_text.sn16.13:0.3: The Counterfeit of the True Teaching
translation_text.sn16.13:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn16.13:1.3: Then Venerable Mahākassapa went up to the Buddha, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to him:
translation_text.sn16.13:1.4: “What is the cause, sir, what is the reason why there used to be fewer training rules but more enlightened mendicants?
translation_text.sn16.13:1.5: And what is the cause, what is the reason why these days there are more training rules and fewer enlightened mendicants?”
translation_text.sn16.13:1.6: “That’s how it is, Kassapa. When sentient beings are in decline and the true teaching is disappearing there are more training rules and fewer enlightened mendicants.
translation_text.sn16.13:1.7: The true teaching doesn’t disappear as long the counterfeit of the true teaching hasn’t appeared in the world.
translation_text.sn16.13:1.8: But when the counterfeit of the true teaching appears in the world then the true teaching disappears.
translation_text.sn16.13:2.1: It’s like native gold, which doesn’t disappear as long as counterfeit gold hasn’t appeared in the world.
translation_text.sn16.13:2.2: But when counterfeit gold appears in the world then native gold disappears.
translation_text.sn16.13:2.3: In the same way, the true teaching doesn’t disappear as long the counterfeit of the true teaching hasn’t appeared in the world.
translation_text.sn16.13:2.4: But when the counterfeit of the true teaching appears in the world then the true teaching disappears.
translation_text.sn16.13:3.1: It’s not the elements of earth, water, fire, or air that make the true teaching disappear.
translation_text.sn16.13:3.2: Rather, it’s the futile people who appear right here that make the true teaching disappear.
translation_text.sn16.13:3.3: The true teaching doesn’t disappear like a ship that sinks all at once.
translation_text.sn16.13:4.1: There are five detrimental things that lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching.
translation_text.sn16.13:4.3: It’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen lack respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and immersion.
translation_text.sn16.13:4.4: These five detrimental things lead to the decline and disappearance of the true teaching.
translation_text.sn16.13:5.1: There are five things that lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.
translation_text.sn16.13:5.3: It’s when the monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen maintain respect and reverence for the Teacher, the teaching, the Saṅgha, the training, and immersion.
translation_text.sn16.13:5.4: These five things lead to the continuation, persistence, and enduring of the true teaching.”
translation_text.sn16.13:6.7: The Linked Discourses with Kassapa are complete.
